tools.description: Execute read-only DuckDB query against MotherDuck databases. For cross-database queries, use fully qualified names: database.schema.table (or database.table for main schema).
tools.name: query

tools.description: List all databases in your MotherDuck account with their names and types.
tools.name: list_databases

tools.description: List all tables and views in a MotherDuck database with their comments.

database.description: Database name to list tables from

schema.description: Schema name (optional, defaults to all schemas)

tools.name: list_tables

tools.description: List all columns of a table or view with their types and comments.
tools.name: list_columns

tools.description: Search the catalog for databases, schemas, tables, columns, and shares using fuzzy matching. Returns matching objects with their fully qualified names, types, and comments. Useful for discovering available data when you don't know exact names.

query.description: Search term to find in object names (supports partial matching, underscores, dots)

object_types.description: Optional filter to limit results to specific object types

tools.name: search_catalog

tools.description: Ask a question about DuckDB or MotherDuck. Returns answers from official documentation.
tools.name: ask_docs_question

tools.description: List all database shares that have been shared with you. Returns share names and URLs.
tools.name: list_shares

tools.description: List all dives in MotherDuck. Dives are interactive React data apps that query live data. Returns metadata including current_version (the latest version number, 1-indexed) for each dive. Use read_dive with the optional version parameter to retrieve a specific historical version. Optionally filter by keywords to search in title and description.

keywords.description: Optional keywords to filter dives by title or description (case-insensitive, all words must match)

tools.name: list_dives

tools.description: Save a new dive to MotherDuck. Returns a URL the user can click to view the dive. Call get_dive_guide first and iterate with the user on the design thoroughly before suggesting to save. Never save without previous confirmation that iteration is done.

description.description: A brief description of the dive

content.description: The JSX/React component code for the dive

tools.name: save_dive

tools.description: Update an existing dive's title, description, or content. Returns a URL the user can click to view the updated dive. At least one field must be provided.

id.description: The unique identifier (UUID) of the dive to update

tools.name: update_dive

tools.description: Read a specific dive by ID, including its full JSX/React component code. Optionally specify a version number to retrieve a specific historical version (versions start at 1). If no version is specified, the latest version is returned.

id.description: The unique identifier of the dive to read

version.description: Optional version number to retrieve (1-indexed). If not specified, returns the latest version.

tools.name: read_dive

tools.description: Delete a dive by ID. This action is permanent and cannot be undone.

id.description: The unique identifier of the dive to delete

tools.name: delete_dive

tools.description: Load instructions for creating MotherDuck dives. Call this before starting to iterate with the user on artifacts, data visualizations, plots, dashboards, etc.

client.description: The client environment requesting the guide. Use 'claude' for Claude web/desktop, 'chatgpt' for ChatGPT, 'claude_code' for Cursor/IDE integrations with local filesystem access, or 'other' for other MCP clients.

tools.name: get_dive_guide

tools.description: Share the data for a dive with your organization. Creates org-scoped shares for owned databases used in the dive, and updates the dive so others in the organization can view it.

diveId.description: The unique identifier (UUID) of the dive

tools.name: share_dive_data

summary.text:
**Exploring dataset options**

I’m thinking about what the user might need, and it seems like they might have a dataset in MotherDuck. I should start by querying to discover which databases or tables are available. I’ll need to use the right tools for that. It feels a bit like sleuthing—searching for the right information. I wonder what I’ll find out! Let's get started on querying those options for the user.

**Inspecting shipment data**

I need to find shipments that have the ship mode set to 'AIR.' That seems like a crucial detail for the task at hand. I’m thinking that this data is probably in the sample_data file. So my next step will be to look closely at the tables involved to confirm that. It's essential to ensure I'm on the right track! Let's get started on inspecting those tables.

It seems like I'm trying to figure out how to handle table listing without a specific schema. The tool I'm using requires a schema, but it also defaults to all schemas—interesting! I'm wondering if passing an empty schema might work, like just using `""`. That could be a solution to get the list I need, even if I don’t have a defined schema. It's all about finding that right approach!

output.name: list_tables

It supports applications like finding related films by description or title and powering content discovery and NLP-driven queries."}, {"schema": "nyc", "name": "rideshare", "type": "table", "comment": "This table captures individual rideshare trips in NYC, including when and where rides were requested, picked up, and dropped off, the trip distance and duration, and associated fares, surcharges, tips, and driver pay. It supports analysis of service operations and performance (e.g., demand patterns, trip logistics, and revenue components) for high-volume for-hire services and their bases."}, {"schema": "nyc", "name": "service_requests", "type": "table", "comment": "This table records New York City 311 service requests, capturing when and where a complaint was made, the responsible agency, issue type and description, status, and resolution details. Its business purpose is to track, manage, and analyze public service issues across the city to support operational response, accountability, and resource planning."}, {"schema": "nyc", "name": "taxi", "type": "table", "comment": "This table captures individual NYC yellow taxi trips, including pickup/dropoff times and locations, passenger counts, trip distance, payment method, and a detailed fare breakdown. It supports operational analysis, revenue auditing, and planning by enabling insights into demand patterns, route usage, pricing, and service performance across the city."}, {"schema": "stackoverflow_survey", "name": "survey_results", "type": "table", "comment": "This table contains responses from Stack Overflow\u2019s annual developer survey, capturing demographics, job status, satisfaction, skills, technologies used and desired, and attitudes toward tools, work, and the Stack Overflow community. Its business purpose is to analyze the developer ecosystem for insights that inform product decisions, hiring trends, community engagement, and market research."}, {"schema": "stackoverflow_survey", "name": "survey_schemas", "type": "table", "comment": null}, {"schema": "who", "name": "ambient_air_quality", "type": "table", "comment": null}], "tableCount": 8, "viewCount": 0}

output.arguments:
{"database":"tpch_10","sql":"WITH x AS (
  SELECT
    p.p_mfgr AS manufacturer,
    p.p_size AS product_size,
    COUNT(*) AS total_lines,
    SUM(CASE WHEN l.l_shipmode IN ('AIR','AIR REG') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS air_lines
  FROM lineitem l
  JOIN part p ON p.p_partkey = l.l_partkey
  GROUP BY 1,2
)
SELECT
  manufacturer,
  product_size,
  total_lines,
  air_lines,
  ROUND(100.0 * air_lines / NULLIF(total_lines,0), 2) AS air_pct
FROM x
WHERE total_lines >= 200
ORDER BY air_pct DESC, air_lines DESC
LIMIT 20;"}
output.name: query

summary.text:
**Evaluating air shipment data**

I'm analyzing the air shipment data, noting that around 14.5% implies an equal distribution among ship modes with no single one dominating. I think I could interpret "typically shipped via air" as referring to the most frequent shipments, not necessarily their overall proportion. It seems important to filter the data further by focusing on air shipments and checking which manufacturers and sizes are common. That will give me better insights!

output.arguments:
{"database":"tpch_10","sql":"SELECT
  p.p_mfgr AS manufacturer,
  p.p_size AS product_size,
  COUNT(*) AS air_shipments
FROM lineitem l
JOIN part p ON p.p_partkey = l.l_partkey
WHERE l.l_shipmode IN ('AIR','AIR REG')
GROUP BY 1,2
ORDER BY air_shipments DESC
LIMIT 20;"}
output.name: query

summary.text:
**Summarizing air shipment details**

I'm thinking I should summarize the top manufacturers in the air shipment industry along with the leading sizes. That way, I can give a clear picture of what's important. I'll work on computing this information to present it effectively. It's essential to separate these two aspects to avoid confusion and provide a straightforward understanding for anyone interested in air shipments. I’ll get started on that!

output.arguments:
{"database":"tpch_10","sql":"WITH air AS (
  SELECT p.p_mfgr AS manufacturer, p.p_size AS product_size
  FROM lineitem l
  JOIN part p ON p.p_partkey = l.l_partkey
  WHERE l.l_shipmode IN ('AIR','AIR REG')
)
SELECT 'manufacturer' AS dimension, manufacturer AS key, COUNT(*) AS air_shipments
FROM air
GROUP BY 1,2
UNION ALL
SELECT 'product_size' AS dimension, CAST(product_size AS VARCHAR) AS key, COUNT(*) AS air_shipments
FROM air
GROUP BY 1,2
ORDER BY dimension, air_shipments DESC;"}
output.name: query

summary.text:
**Computing shipment distribution**

I’m thinking about comparing manufacturers and their shipment sizes, noting there are slight differences. It might be useful to calculate the share of air shipments from each manufacturer by size compared to the overall shipments. But I remember that "typically shipped via air" could mean some items have a higher air shipment share than their total shipments. Earlier data showed around 14%. So, I'll compute the overall share of shipment modes next.

output.arguments:
{"database":"tpch_10","sql":"SELECT
  l_shipmode,
  COUNT(*) AS line_count,
  ROUND(100.0 * COUNT(*) / SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER (), 2) AS pct
FROM lineitem
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY line_count DESC;"}
output.name: query

output.arguments:
{"database":"tpch_10","sql":"SELECT
  p.p_mfgr AS manufacturer,
  p.p_size AS product_size,
  COUNT(*) AS air_shipments
FROM lineitem l
JOIN part p ON p.p_partkey = l.l_partkey
WHERE l.l_shipmode IN ('AIR','REG AIR')
GROUP BY 1,2
ORDER BY air_shipments DESC
LIMIT 20;"}
output.name: query

summary.text: I'm focusing on calculating the top 20 combinations. I need to make sure I compute the air percentage accurately for each combination while considering both shipping modes. Then, I’ll compare these percentages to the overall values. This requires careful attention to detail, so I want to ensure I'm getting it right. Let’s get started on the calculations to provide a clear picture of the results!

output.arguments:
{"database":"tpch_10","sql":"WITH x AS (
  SELECT
    p.p_mfgr AS manufacturer,
    p.p_size AS product_size,
    COUNT(*) AS total_lines,
    SUM(CASE WHEN l.l_shipmode IN ('AIR','REG AIR') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS air_lines
  FROM lineitem l
  JOIN part p ON p.p_partkey = l.l_partkey
  GROUP BY 1,2
)
SELECT
  manufacturer,
  product_size,
  total_lines,
  air_lines,
  ROUND(100.0 * air_lines / total_lines, 2) AS air_pct
FROM x
WHERE total_lines >= 200
ORDER BY air_pct DESC, air_lines DESC
LIMIT 15;"}
output.name: query

summary.text:
**Calculating air share**

I’m looking at the air percentage, which is about 28.7% when I consider both AIR and REG AIR. It seems the overall share should be around 28.57%. The figures appear pretty uniform, which is good! I’ll need to calculate the combined share of air and REG AIR to finalize this. Let’s make sure to double-check the numbers as I continue with the calculations for accuracy!

output.arguments:
{"database":"tpch_10","sql":"SELECT
  ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN l_shipmode IN ('AIR','REG AIR') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*), 2) AS overall_air_pct,
  COUNT(*) AS total_lines
FROM lineitem;"}
output.name: query

summary.text:
**Analyzing air shipment data**

I see that there isn't a standout option, as air usage averages about 28.6% across all combinations. I need to provide the most common manufacturer and size combinations for air shipments along with the overall manufacturer counts. It's also crucial to confirm which manufacturers are the top players for air shipments while ensuring I use the correct shipping modes in my analysis. Let’s compile this information clearly!

output.arguments:
{"database":"tpch_10","sql":"WITH air AS (
  SELECT p.p_mfgr AS manufacturer, p.p_size AS product_size
  FROM lineitem l
  JOIN part p ON p.p_partkey = l.l_partkey
  WHERE l.l_shipmode IN ('AIR','REG AIR')
)
SELECT manufacturer, COUNT(*) AS air_shipments,
       ROUND(100.0*COUNT(*)/SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER (),2) AS share_of_air
FROM air
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY air_shipments DESC;"}
output.name: query

output.arguments:
{"database":"tpch_10","sql":"WITH air AS (
  SELECT p.p_size AS product_size
  FROM lineitem l
  JOIN part p ON p.p_partkey = l.l_partkey
  WHERE l.l_shipmode IN ('AIR','REG AIR')
)
SELECT product_size, COUNT(*) AS air_shipments,
       ROUND(100.0*COUNT(*)/SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER (),2) AS share_of_air
FROM air
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY air_shipments DESC
LIMIT 10;"}
output.name: query

summary.text:
**Crafting a shipping narrative**

I'm looking at the tpch_10 dataset, focusing on air shipments. It seems there's no strong skew since manufacturers account for about 20% of air shipments, with an air share per manufacturer size of around 28.6%. I'll highlight the top combinations by volume, and if the user needs a real operational dataset, we'll need to clarify that. I'll create a table showing the top 10 manufacturer-size combos by air shipments and mention that shipping mode in TPC-H data is somewhat random.

content.text:
Using the `tpch_10` dataset (joining `lineitem` → `part`) and treating **air** as `l_shipmode IN ('AIR','REG AIR')`:

### There aren’t “typical” (i.e., clearly air-preferred) manufacturers/sizes in this data
Air shipping is essentially uniform:
- Overall share shipped via air (AIR + REG AIR): **28.57%** of all line items.
- For manufacturer–size combinations, the air share is also ~**28.7%** (no meaningful standouts).

### Most common manufacturer × product size combinations shipped via air (by volume)
These are simply the highest-count air shipments:

| manufacturer | product_size | air_shipments |
|---|---:|---:|
| Manufacturer#3 | 42 | 71,319 |
| Manufacturer#5 | 3 | 70,950 |
| Manufacturer#4 | 35 | 70,834 |
| Manufacturer#3 | 48 | 70,521 |
| Manufacturer#4 | 20 | 70,306 |
| Manufacturer#5 | 24 | 70,216 |
| Manufacturer#1 | 24 | 70,196 |
| Manufacturer#4 | 13 | 70,154 |
| Manufacturer#2 | 48 | 70,123 |
| Manufacturer#2 | 16 | 70,047 |

### Manufacturers most shipped via air (share of all air shipments)
All are ~20% each (again, no strong skew):
- Manufacturer#3: **3,434,933** (20.04%)
- Manufacturer#5: **3,431,803** (20.03%)
- Manufacturer#4: **3,424,936** (19.99%)
- Manufacturer#1: **3,423,612** (19.98%)
- Manufacturer#2: **3,421,160** (19.96%)

If you’re asking about your own shipping data (where “air” is driven by urgency/value/size), tell me what tables/fields represent **manufacturer**, **product size**, and **ship mode**, and I’ll compute the combinations where air is actually *preferred* (e.g., air share > 50% with a minimum shipment count).
